full_response:
To rotate a point \((x_0, y_0, z_0)\) by an angle \(\theta\) about the origin in the \(xy\)-plane (i.e., about the \(z\)-axis), the coordinates transform as:

\[
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x_0 \cos \theta - y_0 \sin \theta, \\
y' &= x_0 \sin \theta + y_0 \cos \theta, \\
z' &= z_0.
\end{aligned}
\]

This transformation can be expressed in matrix form as \(\mathbf{X}' = R[\theta] \cdot \mathbf{X}\), where \(\mathbf{X} = (x_0, y_0, z_0)^T\) and \(\mathbf{X}' = (x', y', z')^T\). The rotation matrix \(R[\theta]\) is:

\[
R[\theta] = \begin{pmatrix}
\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

\boxed{R[\theta] = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}}

Confidence: 100%
